title: practical english usage - Part 238

In speech, information is generally separated out In writing, information is often packed tightly into complex structures (see put    together 1  spacing      out   information:        a  course       with     three      levels,      it's   carefully 299 spoken      sentence       structure                 _ grammar •  300   avoiding repetition: Wonderful, isn't it?

For more about ’ echo questions’  of this kind, ►    304.

’ I'm getting married.’ ’ You're getting married? Who to?

Repeating somebody else’ s words can show surprise or disbelief.

I'm very very angry. You'll eat every mouthful. Every single mouthful.

be done for emphasis.

Speakers can of course repeat vocabulary and structures deliberately. This can ’ United didn't play very well, then.’ ’ Absolute rubbish.’ ’ Terrible weather.’ ’ Dreadful.’ notusually ’ Glorious day.' ’ Wonderful, isn't it? (                 Glorious, isn't it?) carefully avoided.

thing in other words which  are at least as emphatic. Repetition is gives his/her opinion of something, and the other agrees by saying the same without a good reason. There is a common kind of exchange where one speaker spoken exchanges people often prefer to avoid repeating each other’ s words Speech is more tolerant of repetition than formal writing (►   287), but even in 300 avoiding       repetition:      Wonderful,           isn't    it?

and ►    308  (short answers).

For other uses of tags and similar structures, ►    305-306  (question tags), ►    307  (reply questions) For more about dropping words ('ellipsis’ ) at the beginning of a sentence, ►    277.

(I) don't think much  of the party, myself.

Pronouns are not usually used alone in tags, except for reflexives.

He likes his beer, he does.

You've gone mad, you have. I'm getting fed up, I am.

the subject and verb.

Sometimes a tag gives no new information, but simply repeats and emphasises 4 emphasising         tags:   You've      gone      mad,     you     have.

(She) really got on my nerves, Sylvia did.

(He) hasn't a chance, Fred hasn't. (He) likes his beer, Jack does.

The  tag can repeat the verb by using an auxiliary.

Living in the clouds, you lot. Crazy, that driver.

Hasn't a chance, Fred. Likes his beer, Jack. Very polite, your children.

of this kind, ►    277.) also drop unstressed forms of be. (For more detailed information about ‘ ellipsis’ In sentences like these, we  often drop the pronoun at the beginning. We   can They're very polite, your children.

He hasn't a chance, Fred. He likes his beer, Jack.

in a tag. These structures are more common in British than American English.

It is possible to ‘ announce’  the subject with a pronoun, and put the full subject That's the doorbell, I think. I don't mind, to be honest.

Those kids work very hard, most of them.

Speech and Spoken Exchanges   Section 27 grammar *301   discourse markers in speech Any questions? Right, let's have a word about tomorrow's arrangements.

OK. Now a word about iron oxide. There's two basic types...

introducing                    a   new        subject             (for     example              in    lectures): now; all right; up on the roof and looks at the tiles ...

. .. on the roof - Jeremy, put the cat down, please. As I was saying if Jack gets returning                to    previous               subject: as I was saying she's got a new job? And she said that Jake told her...

I was talking to Annie yesterday. Incidentally / By the way, did you know brief         change             of    subject: incidentally; by the way To start with, let me remind you that I'm the one who's paying for all this.

place, I haven't got the time. And then I'm going to be away all next week.

I don't think I can help you. First(ly) / First of all / For one thing / In the first for one thing* and then subdividing: first; firstly; first of all; in the first place; to start with; I've invited Andy and Joseph. As for Joshua, I never want to see him  again.
